1910s.economic_conditions: The decade began with progressive reform and ended with wartime prosperity. Wilson's reforms improved working conditions but did not satisfy labor radicals. The Paterson Strike of 1913 shut down the silk industry for months. World War I created insatiable demand for manufactured goods, chemicals, and munitions. New Jersey's factories ran around the clock. Wartime prosperity brought full employment but also inflation.
dominant_industries: chemical production (beginning of NJ's chemical corridor)
1910s.migration_patterns: European immigration continued until the war disrupted transatlantic travel. The Great Migration brought increasing numbers of Black southerners to New Jersey's cities, particularly Newark, Camden, and Trenton. Wartime labor shortages drew women and minorities into factory work. Southern and Eastern European immigration would resume briefly after the war before quota laws restricted it in the 1920s.
1910s.social_context: The Paterson Strike of 1913 became a national cause celebre, with the IWW, socialist intellectuals, and Greenwich Village artists rallying to the workers' cause. A famous pageant at Madison Square Garden dramatized the strikers' plight. Wartime patriotism suppressed dissent — German-Americans faced suspicion and harassment. The Spanish Flu killed thousands and overwhelmed hospitals. Women's suffrage movements gained momentum.
1910s.daily_life_snapshot: A silk weaver in Paterson joined the 1913 picket line, marching past armed police with thousands of fellow workers, singing labor songs in Italian, English, and Yiddish. His family survived on strike fund payments and neighborhood charity. The sound of his silent loom was louder than any protest — it meant the bosses were losing money too.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Paterson Silk Strike of 1913: months of hardship, police violence, and eventual defeat for workers
World War I: over 3,000 New Jersey soldiers killed; many more wounded or gassed
Spanish Flu pandemic (1918–1919): thousands dead, public gatherings banned, hospitals overwhelmed
Black Tom explosion (July 30, 1916): massive munitions explosion on Jersey City waterfront caused damage across the region
narrative_hooks: A Paterson silk weaver walks the picket line in 1913, knowing his family may starve but unwilling to return to the looms at starvation wages
A soldier at Camp Dix writes his mother a letter before shipping out to France, promising to come home safe
The Black Tom explosion shatters windows across Jersey City and Lower Manhattan, reminding everyone that the war has reached American soil
A nurse in Newark tends Spanish Flu patients stacked in hospital hallways, wearing a gauze mask that does little to stop the disease

key_events: Prohibition begins (1920) — New Jersey's coast becomes a major rum-running corridor
Women gain the right to vote with the 19th Amendment (1920)
Holland Tunnel opens, connecting Jersey City to Manhattan (1927)
RCA establishes headquarters in Camden; radio broadcasting revolution begins
Immigration quotas (1921, 1924) drastically reduce European immigration
Garden State becomes a suburban frontier as automobiles enable commuting
Population exceeds 3.1 million (1920 census)
1920s.economic_conditions: The Roaring Twenties brought prosperity to much of New Jersey. Manufacturing boomed. The automobile and new highways transformed the landscape as suburbs spread. RCA in Camden pioneered radio technology and broadcasting. Prohibition created a vast illegal economy — rum-running along the Jersey Shore was enormously profitable. Atlantic City entered its golden age as a resort and convention destination.
dominant_industries: chemical manufacturing (corridor along the Turnpike route)
1920s.migration_patterns: Immigration quotas drastically curtailed European immigration after 1924. The Great Migration of Black southerners to New Jersey accelerated, with large communities forming in Newark, Camden, and other cities. Internal migration from farms to suburbs increased as automobile ownership spread. Suburban development began reshaping the state's population distribution.
1920s.social_context: Prohibition defined the decade's social life — speakeasies, bootleggers, and organized crime flourished. Atlantic City hosted the famous 1929 conference where crime bosses divided territories. The Ku Klux Klan experienced a brief resurgence in New Jersey, targeting Catholics, Jews, and Blacks. Jazz, radio, and motion pictures transformed popular culture. Women entered the workforce and public life in greater numbers.
1920s.daily_life_snapshot: A family in a new bungalow in a Middlesex County suburb listened to a radio program on a set manufactured in Camden, the tinny voices and music filling the living room. Father drove to his factory job in a Model T. Mother joined a women's club and planted a garden. On summer weekends they piled into the car for the shore, joining a line of automobiles stretching to the horizon.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Prohibition-related violence and organized crime corruption
Ku Klux Klan rallies and anti-Catholic, anti-Jewish, and anti-Black intimidation
Silk industry decline in Paterson as rayon and synthetic fabrics emerged
Stock market crash (October 1929) ended the boom and foreshadowed the Depression
narrative_hooks: A rum-runner guides a boat through the fog off the Jersey Shore, cases of Canadian whiskey stacked in the hold, dodging Coast Guard cutters
An RCA engineer in Camden tests a new radio receiver, marveling that a voice in New York can be heard in living rooms across the state
A Black family from Georgia arrives at Newark's Penn Station, carrying cardboard suitcases and the hope that the North will be different

key_events: Great Depression devastates New Jersey's industrial economy; unemployment reaches 30% in some cities
Lindbergh baby kidnapping from Hopewell, New Jersey shocks the nation (March 1, 1932)
Bruno Hauptmann tried and executed for the Lindbergh kidnapping (1935–1936)
Hindenburg disaster: airship explodes while landing at Lakehurst Naval Air Station, killing 36 (May 6, 1937)
New Deal programs (WPA, CCC, PWA) employ thousands in New Jersey infrastructure projects
Population exceeds 4.0 million (1930 census)
1930s.economic_conditions: The Depression hit New Jersey's industrial cities hard. Factory closures, bank failures, and mass unemployment devastated working-class communities. Newark, Paterson, Camden, and Trenton saw unemployment rates above 25%. New Deal programs provided some relief through public works projects — building roads, bridges, parks, and public buildings. Agriculture in South Jersey suffered from low crop prices.
dominant_industries: chemical manufacturing (surviving the Depression better than most)
1930s.migration_patterns: The Depression slowed all migration. European immigration was already restricted by quotas. Black migration from the South continued at a reduced pace. Some unemployed workers left New Jersey for other regions. Internal migration shifted as families moved in with relatives, doubled up in housing, or returned to rural areas from cities. Hoovervilles and shanty towns appeared in vacant lots.
1930s.social_context: The Depression tested New Jersey's social fabric severely. Bread lines, soup kitchens, and Hoovervilles became common sights. Labor unions gained strength as workers organized for survival. Political machines provided patronage and relief in exchange for votes. The Lindbergh kidnapping and Hindenburg disaster made New Jersey front-page news for tragic reasons. Radio became the dominant entertainment and news medium.
1930s.daily_life_snapshot: An unemployed factory worker in Newark stood in a bread line outside a church, hat in hand, waiting for a bowl of soup and a piece of bread. His children wore hand-me-downs and cardboard in their shoes. The house was cold — they could not afford coal. At night, the family gathered around the radio, the one luxury they could not bear to sell.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Great Depression: mass unemployment, factory closures, bank failures, homelessness
Lindbergh kidnapping (1932) and trial traumatized the nation
Hindenburg disaster at Lakehurst (1937) killed 36 and ended the airship era
Dust and despair in industrial cities; child malnutrition documented by social workers
narrative_hooks: An unemployed Paterson silk worker sells apples on a street corner, his calloused weaver's hands now holding a fruit crate
A radio announcer at Lakehurst screams 'Oh, the humanity!' as the Hindenburg erupts in flame above the landing field
A WPA worker paves a road in Morris County, grateful for three dollars a day and the dignity of work

key_events: World War II transforms New Jersey into an 'Arsenal of Democracy' — shipyards, munitions plants, and military bases run at full capacity
Fort Dix, Camp Kilmer, and Picatinny Arsenal are major military installations
New York Shipbuilding Corporation in Camden builds battleships, aircraft carriers, and cruisers
Women enter the workforce in unprecedented numbers — 'Rosie the Riveter' at Jersey factories
V-J Day celebrations (August 1945); soldiers return home
New Jersey Turnpike authorized (1948); construction begins
Population exceeds 4.1 million (1940 census)
1940s.economic_conditions: World War II ended the Depression overnight. Every factory in New Jersey ran at capacity producing war materiel. Shipyards in Camden and Kearny built warships. Chemical plants produced explosives and synthetic materials. Full employment returned, wages rose, and women filled jobs vacated by soldiers. Post-war, the economy transitioned to consumer goods with remarkable speed, aided by pent-up demand and returning veterans' spending power.
1940s.migration_patterns: The war accelerated the Great Migration — large numbers of Black workers moved from the South to New Jersey's industrial cities for war production jobs. Women of all backgrounds entered the industrial workforce. After the war, returning veterans used the GI Bill to buy homes in suburbs, beginning the great suburban expansion. Puerto Rican migration to New Jersey also began in earnest.
1940s.social_context: The war united New Jersey's diverse ethnic communities in common purpose. Italian-Americans, German-Americans, and others demonstrated patriotism despite facing suspicion. Black workers gained factory jobs but faced persistent discrimination in housing and promotion. Post-war, the GI Bill transformed opportunities for veterans — college education, homeownership, and small business loans became accessible. Suburban development began reshaping the state.
1940s.daily_life_snapshot: A woman riveter at the Camden shipyard climbed scaffolding on an aircraft carrier hull, her welding mask flipped up, lunch pail in hand. The din of riveting guns, the flash of arc welders, and the smell of hot metal filled the enormous dry dock. She earned more than she ever had before and knew the job would end when the men came home.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Over 12,000 New Jersey service members killed in World War II
Racial discrimination in housing and employment persisted despite wartime rhetoric of equality
Post-war housing shortage as returning veterans overwhelmed available homes
Industrial pollution accumulated from wartime production with no environmental regulation
narrative_hooks: A woman welder at the Camden shipyard helps build an aircraft carrier, knowing her brother is somewhere in the Pacific waiting for it
A Black machinist from Alabama works alongside white workers at a Bayonne factory for the first time — the war has opened doors, but not all of them
A soldier steps off a train at Camp Kilmer, duffel bag on his shoulder, ready to ship out and unsure if he will return to this New Jersey platform

key_events: New Jersey Turnpike opens (1951), connecting northern and southern New Jersey and transforming transportation
Garden State Parkway opens (1954), linking the Shore to the northern suburbs
Suburban development explodes — Levittown-style communities spread across the state
Newark and other cities begin losing population to suburbs (early white flight)
Pharmaceutical industry grows: Johnson & Johnson, Merck, and others anchor 'Pharma Alley'
Salk polio vaccine tested; New Jersey children among the first vaccinated
Population exceeds 4.8 million (1950 census)
1950s.economic_conditions: The 1950s were a golden age for middle-class New Jersey. Suburban homebuilding created a construction boom. The Turnpike and Parkway opened enormous areas to development. Corporate headquarters relocated from New York City to New Jersey suburbs, bringing white-collar jobs. The pharmaceutical industry grew rapidly. But the seeds of urban decline were planted as cities lost residents and tax base to suburbs.
1950s.migration_patterns: White middle-class families fled cities for suburbs, aided by the GI Bill, new highways, and racial discrimination in housing (redlining). Black and Puerto Rican populations grew in Newark, Camden, Trenton, and other cities as whites departed. Corporate relocation brought professional workers to suburban office parks. The Puerto Rican community grew significantly in Newark, Hoboken, and Passaic.
1950s.social_context: Suburbanization defined the decade. New Jersey became the archetype of suburban America — ranch houses, station wagons, shopping centers, and commuter rail. But racial segregation in housing was pervasive: redlining, restrictive covenants, and outright discrimination confined minorities to urban cores. Urban schools deteriorated while suburban schools thrived. The civil rights movement began to stir.
1950s.daily_life_snapshot: A family in a new split-level in Middlesex County ate TV dinners on tray tables while watching 'I Love Lucy,' the children doing homework on the living room floor. On Saturdays, father mowed the lawn, mother drove to the new shopping center, and the whole family might drive to the Shore on the Parkway. The smell of cut grass and backyard barbecue defined summer.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Urban decline accelerated as white flight drained cities of residents and tax revenue
Racial discrimination in housing confined Black and Puerto Rican families to deteriorating neighborhoods
Industrial pollution accumulated in the chemical corridor, rivers, and coastal areas — largely unregulated
Korean War: New Jersey soldiers served and died (1950–1953)
narrative_hooks: A family drives the brand-new New Jersey Turnpike for the first time, marveling at the smooth road and the speed, the old state receding in the rearview mirror
A Black family trying to buy a house in a new suburb is steered back to the city by a real estate agent who says the neighborhood 'wouldn't be right for them'
A Merck chemist in a Rahway laboratory tests a compound that could save millions of lives, the quiet drama of pharmaceutical research

key_events: Newark riots (July 1967) — five days of civil unrest leave 26 dead, hundreds injured, and the city scarred for decades
Civil rights movement reaches New Jersey; protests against housing discrimination and police brutality
Vietnam War: New Jersey sends tens of thousands of soldiers; anti-war protests grow at Rutgers and Princeton
Immigration and Nationality Act of 1965 opens immigration from Asia, Latin America, and Africa
Meadowlands Sports Complex planned; Interstate highway system nears completion
Population exceeds 6.0 million (1960 census)
1960s.economic_conditions: New Jersey's economy split between thriving suburbs and declining cities. Pharmaceutical, chemical, and defense industries generated wealth in suburban corridors. Urban cores lost manufacturing jobs as factories moved south or overseas. Newark's economic collapse preceded the 1967 riots. The defense industry profited from Vietnam. New corporate campuses rose along Route 1 and I-287.
dominant_industries: electronics and telecommunications (Bell Labs in Murray Hill)
1960s.migration_patterns: The 1965 Immigration Act opened New Jersey to immigrants from India, Korea, Cuba, the Philippines, and other countries, beginning the state's transformation into one of the most diverse in the nation. White flight continued from cities to suburbs and exurbs. Black communities in Newark, Camden, and Trenton grew as Southern migration continued. Puerto Rican communities expanded in northern New Jersey cities.
1960s.social_context: The Newark riots were a watershed moment, exposing decades of racial injustice, police brutality, housing discrimination, and economic neglect. The riots accelerated white and corporate flight from Newark and other cities. The Vietnam War divided communities along generational lines. The counterculture and civil rights movements challenged established norms. New Jersey began grappling with its industrial pollution legacy.
1960s.daily_life_snapshot: A teenager in Newark in the summer of 1967 heard gunfire from his apartment window and watched National Guard troops patrol his neighborhood in armored vehicles. The acrid smell of smoke from burning buildings, the wail of sirens, and the crunch of broken glass underfoot were the sensory memories of a city in crisis. His family huddled indoors, unsure if the world outside would ever feel safe again.
notable_disasters_or_hardships: Newark riots (July 1967): 26 dead, 725 injured, $10 million in property damage
Vietnam War casualties: over 1,400 New Jersey service members killed
Accelerating urban decay in Newark, Camden, Trenton, and other cities
Industrial pollution — rivers, marshes, and soil contaminated by decades of unregulated dumping
Racial tensions and housing discrimination remained entrenched despite civil rights legislation
narrative_hooks: A Newark mother pulls her children away from the window as National Guard bullets shatter the glass in July 1967
A Bell Labs engineer in Murray Hill works on a breakthrough that will change communications forever, oblivious to the turmoil just miles away
A Vietnamese refugee family arrives at Newark Airport, beginning a new life in a state that is itself being remade by change
A college student at Rutgers marches against the Vietnam War, torn between patriotism and conscience
